Regression Project: Boston House Price Prediction

Objective

The problem at hand is to predict the housing prices of a town or a suburb based on the features of the locality provided to us. In the process, we will identify the most important features affecting the price of the house and employ techniques of data preprocessing and build a linear regression model that predicts the prices for the unseen data.

In [55]:
# Import libraries for data manipulation
import pandas as pd

import numpy as np

# Import libraries for data visualization
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

import seaborn as sns

from statsmodels.graphics.gofplots import ProbPlot

# Import libraries for building linear regression model
from statsmodels.formula.api import ols

import statsmodels.api as sm

from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression

# Import library for preparing data
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split

# Import library for data preprocessing
from sklearn.preprocessing import MinMaxScaler

import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")

Observation from this dataset:

• All 13 columns have 506 non-null values, consistent with no missing data

• CRIM: is highly right skewed with mean (3.71) far over the median (0.26), indicating a small number of towns with very high crime rates

• ZN: median 0 indicates that over half the towns have no land zoned for large residential lots and the mean (11.4) is pulled by a few high-value outliers that has lots over 25,000 square feet

• CHAS: a binary variable with a mean of (0.069) indicates that only about 7% of tracts border the Charles River.

• NOX: ranges from 0.385 to 0.871, values fairly evenly spread with mean and median closer to ~0.55, indicating the Nitric Oxide concentration is fairly evenly spread (parts per 10 million).

However, this interpretation of data alone does not conclude that the pollution is much concentrated near industrial zone, which the later analysis reveals.

• RM: mean and median are roughly close around ~6.2, suggesting symmetric distribution centered around 6 rooms per dwelling

• AGE: median (77.5) is much higher than the means suggests that most towns have a large proportion of owner-occupied units/homes built before 1940

• DIS: right skewed, with most values between 2.1 and 5.2, but a max of 12.1 indicating some towns are much farther from employment centers

• RAD: show a large jump between mean/median and the 75% and max, likely to suggest that a subset of towns closer to the highway share the same top accessibility, while the rest are spread across lower values representing less direct highway access

• TAX: shows a large jump between mean/median to 75th percentile and max, indicating that a meaningful share of towns has tax rates roughly double the typical rate, then the properties taxed at (296 per $10000)

• PTRATIO: Pupil-teacher ratio by town, seem to have a fairly tight distribution with mean and median close to each other (~19)

*Even though this data alone does not act as a significant indicator of home value, running a correlation matrix with other dataset reveals that every additional student per teacher ratio is associated with a ~4.9% drop in median home value, which later analysis reveals *

• LSTAT: right skewed, mean higher than median, and max of ~38, indicate some areas have a high percentage of lower status population

• MEDV: the value ranges from 5 to 50, with mean and median fairly close, indicating the mean/median value of the owner-occupied homes are in the ($22,000) range but the max value of 50 suggests the value of owner-occupied homes caps at (50,000)

Exploratory Data Analysis and Data Preprocessing

Univariate Analysis

In [61]:
# Plotting all the columns to look at their distributions
for i in df.columns:

    plt.figure(figsize = (7, 4))
    sns.histplot(data = df, x = i, kde = True)
    plt.show()

Observation on the graph:

The graphs are consistent with the observation made from the data statistics

Right Skewed: CRIM, ZN, CHAS, DIS, LSTAT

Approximately Normal (Symmetric): RM, PTRATIO

Left-Skewed: AGE

Bimodal or Irregular: RAD, TAS, NOX, INDUS

Approximately Uniform with a Cap: MEDV

As the dependent variable is sightly skewed, we will apply a log transformation on the 'MEDV' column and check the distribution of the transformed column.

In [62]:
df['MEDV_log'] = np.log(df['MEDV'])
In [63]:
sns.histplot(data = df, x = 'MEDV_log', kde = True)
Out[63]:
<Axes: xlabel='MEDV_log', ylabel='Count'>

Observation:

The log-transformed variable (MEDV_log) appears to have a nearly normal distribution without skew, and hence we can proceed.

Bivariate Analysis

Let's check the correlation using the heatmap

In [64]:
 ####Seaborn function to Visualize correlation matrix as a color-coded grid
 ###df.corr computes pairwise correlation coefficients between numerical columns,
 ## producing the matrix that gets passed into the heatmap
plt.figure(figsize = (12, 8))
cmap = sns.diverging_palette(230, 20, as_cmap = True)
sns.heatmap(df.corr(), annot = True, fmt = '.2f', cmap = cmap)
plt.show()

Observation:

Strong Positive Correlations (dark orange, >=0.70)

• RAD & TAX: towns with better highway access tend to have much higher property taxes

• INDUS & NOX: more industrial towns have significantly higher pollution levels

• AGE & NOX: older housing stock is associated with higher pollution, likely because older towns are more industrialized

Strong Negative Correlations:(dark blue, <=-0.70)

• DIS & NOX: towns farther from employment centers have noticeably lower pollution

• DIS & AGE: towns farther from the city have newer homes

• DIS & INDUS: farther towns have less industrial activity

• LSTAT & MEDV: the strongest predictor of house price; neighborhoods with lower-status residents have significantly lower home values

• RM & LSTAT: areas with larger homes tend to have fewer lower-status residents

Moderate Correlations with MEDV:

• RM: more rooms mean higher prices; strongest positive driver of house value

• PTRATIO: higher pupil-teacher ratios (worse school quality) are associated with lower prices

• CRIM: higher crime modestly reduces home values

• CHAS: river proximity has only a weak positive correlation, though it was statistically significant in the model

Multicollinearity Concerns:

• RAD & RAX, INDUS & NOX, NOX & AGE: are all pairs where both variables carry largely redundant information, which is exactly why VIF (Variance Inflation Factor) analysis and variable dropping was necessary before modeling

CHAS: stands out as essentially uncorrelated with almost every other variable, confirming it behaves independently as a binary location indicator than being driven by neighborhood characteristics.

Visualizing the relationship between the features having significant correlations (>= 0.7 or <= -0.7)

In [65]:
# Scatterplot to visualize the relationship between AGE and DIS

plt.show()

Observations:

The distance of the houses to the Boston employment centers appears to decrease moderately as the the proportion of the old houses increase in the town. It is possible that the Boston employment centers are located in the established towns where proportion of owner-occupied units built prior to 1940 is comparatively high.

In [71]:
# Scatterplot to visualize the relationship between INDUS and NOX
plt.figure(figsize = (6, 6))

sns.scatterplot(x = 'INDUS', y = 'NOX', data = df)

plt.show()

Observation:

• There is a clear positive relationship between INDUS and NOX — as the proportion of non-retail business (industrial) acreage increases, nitric oxide concentration rises alongside it

• The relationship is not perfectly linear; there appear to be two clusters — one group of towns with low INDUS (0–10%) and low NOX, and another with high INDUS (around 18%) and correspondingly high NOX, consistent with the bimodal distributions we noted earlier for both variables

• This makes intuitive sense — industrial activity is a primary source of air pollution, so towns with more industrial land use naturally exhibit higher pollution levels

• The correlation of 0.76 from the heatmap is clearly visible here as a strong upward trend

In [72]:
# Scatterplot to visualize the relationship between AGE and NOX
plt.figure(figsize = (6, 6))

sns.scatterplot(x = 'AGE', y = 'NOX', data = df)

plt.show()

Observation:

• There is a moderate positive relationship between AGE and NOX — towns with a higher proportion of older homes (built before 1940) tend to have higher nitric oxide concentrations, consistent with the heatmap correlation of 0.73

• The relationship is more scattered compared to INDUS vs NOX, meaning AGE alone is not as clean a predictor of pollution as industrial activity is

• There are two visible bands in the plot — a lower NOX band (roughly 0.4–0.55) and an upper NOX band (roughly 0.6–0.87), again reflecting the bimodal nature of NOX noted earlier. Within each band, the upward trend with AGE is still visible

• The explanation is logical — older, more established towns tend to have been industrialized for longer, accumulating more pollution-generating infrastructure over time, whereas newer suburban developments farther from the city core tend to have both younger housing stock and cleaner air

In [73]:
# Scatterplot to visualize the relationship between DIS and NOX
plt.figure(figsize = (6, 6))

sns.scatterplot(x = 'DIS', y = 'NOX', data = df)

plt.show()

Observation:

• There is a clear negative relationship between DIS and NOX — as the weighted distance from Boston employment centers increases, nitric oxide concentration decreases

• The relationship appears non-linear — NOX drops steeply as distance increases from 1 to about 5, then flattens out at lower pollution levels beyond that point, suggesting a diminishing effect of distance on pollution reduction

• Most observations are clustered at shorter distances (DIS between 1 and 5), with fewer towns located far from employment centers, reflecting the urban density of the Boston metro area

• This is arguably the most meaningful of the three NOX scatterplots because distance to employment centers acts as a proxy for urbanization level, tying together the effects of industrial activity, housing age, and pollution into one variable.

Split the dataset

Let's split the data into the dependent and independent variables and further split it into train and test set in a ratio of 70:30 for train and test sets.

In [74]:
# Separate the dependent variable and indepedent variables
Y = df['MEDV_log']

X = df.drop(columns = {'MEDV', 'MEDV_log'})

# Add the intercept term
X = sm.add_constant(X)
In [75]:
# splitting the data in 70:30 ratio of train to test data
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, Y, test_size = 0.30, random_state = 1)

Check for Multicollinearity

We will use the Variance Inflation Factor (VIF), to check if there is multicollinearity in the data.

Features having a VIF score > 5 will be dropped / treated till all the features have a VIF score < 5

In [76]:
from statsmodels.stats.outliers_influence import variance_inflation_factor

# Function to check VIF
def checking_vif(train):
    vif = pd.DataFrame()
    vif["feature"] = train.columns

    # Calculating VIF for each feature
    vif["VIF"] = [
        variance_inflation_factor(train.values, i) for i in range(len(train.columns))
    ]
    return vif


print(checking_vif(X_train))

    feature         VIF
0     const  535.372593
1      CRIM    1.924114
2        ZN    2.743574
3     INDUS    3.999538
4      CHAS    1.076564
5       NOX    4.396157
6        RM    1.860950
7       AGE    3.150170
8       DIS    4.355469
9       RAD    8.345247
10      TAX   10.191941
11  PTRATIO    1.943409
12    LSTAT    2.861881

Observation:

• There are two variables with high VIF - RAD and TAX (greater than 5)

• Let's remove TAX as it has the highest VIF values and check the multicollinearity again

In [77]:
# Create the model after dropping TAX
X_train = X_train.drop(columns = ['TAX'])  ## Complete the code

# Check for VIF
print(checking_vif(X_train))

    feature         VIF
0     const  532.025529
1      CRIM    1.923159
2        ZN    2.483399
3     INDUS    3.270983
4      CHAS    1.050708
5       NOX    4.361847
6        RM    1.857918
7       AGE    3.149005
8       DIS    4.333734
9       RAD    2.942862
10  PTRATIO    1.909750
11    LSTAT    2.860251

Now, we will create the linear regression model as the VIF is less than 5 for all the independent variables, and we can assume that multicollinearity has been removed between the variables.

In [78]:
# Create the model
model1 = sm.OLS(y_train, X_train).fit()  # Write your code here

# Get the model summary
model1.summary()
Out[78]:
OLS Regression Results

coef std err t P>|t| [0.025 0.975]
const 4.6324 0.243 19.057 0.000 4.154 5.111
CRIM -0.0128 0.002 -7.445 0.000 -0.016 -0.009
ZN 0.0010 0.001 1.425 0.155 -0.000 0.002
INDUS -0.0004 0.003 -0.148 0.883 -0.006 0.005
CHAS 0.1196 0.039 3.082 0.002 0.043 0.196
NOX -1.0598 0.187 -5.675 0.000 -1.427 -0.692
RM 0.0532 0.021 2.560 0.011 0.012 0.094
AGE 0.0003 0.001 0.461 0.645 -0.001 0.002
DIS -0.0503 0.010 -4.894 0.000 -0.071 -0.030
RAD 0.0076 0.002 3.699 0.000 0.004 0.012
PTRATIO -0.0452 0.007 -6.659 0.000 -0.059 -0.032
LSTAT -0.0298 0.002 -12.134 0.000 -0.035 -0.025
Omnibus: 30.699 Durbin-Watson: 1.923
Prob(Omnibus): 0.000 Jarque-Bera (JB): 83.718
Skew: 0.372 Prob(JB): 6.62e-19

Notes:
[1] Standard Errors assume that the covariance matrix of the errors is correctly specified.
[2] The condition number is large, 2.09e+03. This might indicate that there are
strong multicollinearity or other numerical problems.

Observation:

Overall Model Fit

• R-squared = 0.769 and Adjusted R-squared = 0.761 — the model explains about 76-77% of the variation in log (MEDV), which is a reasonably strong fit

• The F-statistic p-value ≈ 1.4e-101 confirms the overall model is highly statistically significant

Significant Predictors:

Insignificant Predictors (p-value > 0.05)

• ZN (p = 0.155), INDUS (p = 0.883), and AGE (p = 0.645) are not statistically significant at α = 0.05 — their coefficients are not meaningfully different from zero and they do not contribute to explaining house prices once other variables are accounted for

• These three variables will be dropped in the next iteration (model2) to produce a more parsimonious and reliable model.

Examining the significance of the model

It is not enough to fit a multiple regression model to the data, it is necessary to check whether all the regression coefficients are significant or not. Significance here means whether the population regression parameters are significantly different from zero.

From the above it may be noted that the regression coefficients corresponding to ZN, AGE, and INDUS are not statistically significant at level α = 0.05. In other words, the regression coefficients corresponding to these three are not significantly different from 0 in the population. Hence, we will eliminate the three features and create a new model.

In [79]:
# Create the model after dropping columns 'MEDV', 'MEDV_log', 'TAX', 'ZN', 'AGE', 'INDUS' from df DataFrame
Y = df['MEDV_log']

X = df.drop(columns = ['MEDV', 'MEDV_log', 'TAX', 'ZN', 'AGE', 'INDUS'])  # Complete the code

X = sm.add_constant(X)

# Splitting the data in 70:30 ratio of train to test data
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, Y, test_size = 0.30, random_state = 1)

# Create the model
model2 = sm.OLS(y_train, X_train).fit()  ## Complete the code

# Get the model summary
model2.summary()
Out[79]:
OLS Regression Results
Dep. Variable: MEDV_log R-squared: 0.767
Model: OLS Adj. R-squared: 0.762
Method: Least Squares F-statistic: 142.1
Date: Tue, 16 Jun 2026 Prob (F-statistic): 2.61e-104
Time: 01:24:01 Log-Likelihood: 75.486
No. Observations: 354 AIC: -133.0
Df Residuals: 345 BIC: -98.15
Df Model: 8
Covariance Type: nonrobust
coef std err t P>|t| [0.025 0.975]
const 4.6494 0.242 19.242 0.000 4.174 5.125
CRIM -0.0125 0.002 -7.349 0.000 -0.016 -0.009
CHAS 0.1198 0.039 3.093 0.002 0.044 0.196
NOX -1.0562 0.168 -6.296 0.000 -1.386 -0.726
RM 0.0589 0.020 2.928 0.004 0.019 0.098
DIS -0.0441 0.008 -5.561 0.000 -0.060 -0.028
RAD 0.0078 0.002 3.890 0.000 0.004 0.012
PTRATIO -0.0485 0.006 -7.832 0.000 -0.061 -0.036
LSTAT -0.0293 0.002 -12.949 0.000 -0.034 -0.025
Omnibus: 32.514 Durbin-Watson: 1.925
Prob(Omnibus): 0.000 Jarque-Bera (JB): 87.354
Skew: 0.408 Prob(JB): 1.07e-19
Kurtosis: 5.293 Cond. No. 690.


Notes:
[1] Standard Errors assume that the covariance matrix of the errors is correctly specified.

Model Summary:

Overall Model Fit:

• R-squared = 0.767 and Adjusted R-squared = 0.762 — virtually identical to model1 (0.769 / 0.761), confirming that dropping ZN, INDUS, and AGE caused no meaningful loss of explanatory power while making the model simpler and more interpretable

• The Adjusted R-squared actually improved slightly from 0.761 to 0.762, which is expected — removing non-contributing variables reduces the penalty term in the adjusted R² formula

• F-statistic p-value ≈ 2.61e-104 — the overall model remains highly significant.

• LSTAT and NOX remain the strongest drivers of house price in terms of magnitude

• model2 is the final, clean model — all 8 predictors are statistically significant, multicollinearity has been addressed, and explanatory power is preserved. We can now proceed to check the regression assumptions

Checking the below linear regression assumptions

  1. Mean of residuals should be 0
  2. No Heteroscedasticity
  3. Linearity of variables
  4. Normality of error terms

1. Check for mean residuals

In [80]:
residuals = model2.resid

np.mean(residuals)
Out[80]:
np.float64(-2.5303049047106675e-15)

Observation:

• model2.resid extracts the residuals from the fitted model — these are the differences between the actual observed values of log(MEDV) and the values predicted by the model for each observation in the training set

• The mean of the residuals is approximately -1.25e-15, which is effectively 0

• This satisfies the first linear regression assumption — the mean of residuals must equal zero, confirming that the model does not systematically over-predict or under-predict across the training data and that the regression line is correctly centered through the data

2. Check for homoscedasticity

Homoscedasticity - If the residuals are symmetrically distributed across the regression line, then the data is said to be homoscedastic.

Heteroscedasticity- - If the residuals are not symmetrically distributed across the regression line, then the data is said to be heteroscedastic. In this case, the residuals can form a funnel shape or any other non-symmetrical shape.

We'll use Goldfeldquandt Test to test the following hypothesis with alpha = 0.05:

Null hypothesis: Residuals are homoscedastic Alternate hypothesis: Residuals have heteroscedastic

In [81]:
from statsmodels.stats.diagnostic import het_white

from statsmodels.compat import lzip

import statsmodels.stats.api as sms
In [82]:
name = ["F statistic", "p-value"]

test = sms.het_goldfeldquandt(residuals, X_train)  ## Complete the code

lzip(name, test)
Out[82]:
[('F statistic', np.float64(1.0835082923425288)),
 ('p-value', np.float64(0.30190120067668275))]

Observation:

• The Goldfeld-Quandt test checks the following hypothesis:

o Null Hypothesis (H₀): Residuals are homoscedastic (constant variance)

o Alternate Hypothesis (H₁): Residuals are heteroscedastic (non-constant variance)

• The test results are:

o F-statistic ≈ 1.084

o p-value ≈ 0.302

• Since the p-value (0.302) > α (0.05), we fail to reject the null hypothesis, concluding that the residuals are homoscedastic — the variance of the residuals is constant across all levels of the fitted values

• This satisfies the second linear regression assumption, meaning the model's errors are evenly spread and do not fan out or shrink as predicted values increase, which is important for ensuring that the standard errors of the coefficients are reliable and the model's predictions are equally trustworthy across the full range of house prices

3. Linearity of variables

  1. It states that the predictor variables must have a linear relation with the dependent variable.

  2. To test the assumption, we'll plot residuals and the fitted values on a plot and ensure that residuals do not form a strong pattern. They should be randomly and uniformly scattered on the x-axis.

In [83]:
# Predicted values
fitted = model2.fittedvalues

# sns.set_style("whitegrid")
sns.residplot(x = fitted, y = residuals, color = "lightblue", lowess = True)  ## Complete the code

plt.xlabel("Fitted Values")

plt.ylabel("Residual")

plt.title("Residual PLOT")

plt.show()

Observation:

• The residuals appear randomly and symmetrically scattered around the horizontal line at 0 across the full range of fitted values, with no obvious funnel shape, curve, or systematic pattern, an ideal outcome

• The lowess (locally weighted smoothing) line stays close to 0 throughout, with only minor deviations at the extreme ends of the fitted value range, suggesting the linearity assumption holds reasonably well across the middle of the distribution

• There is a slight uptick at the lower end of fitted values and a minor dip at the higher end, indicating the model may marginally underpredict for the lowest-priced homes and marginally overpredict for the highest-priced homes, which is consistent with the known MEDV cap at $50,000 creating some distortion at the upper end

• Overall, this plot satisfies the third linear regression assumption, that the predictor variables have a linear relationship with the dependent variable and confirms that a linear model is an appropriate choice for this data.

4. Normality of error terms

The residuals should be normally distributed.

In [84]:
# Plot histogram of residuals
sns.histplot(residuals, kde = True)
plt.title("Histogram of Residuals")
plt.xlabel("Residuals")
plt.ylabel("Frequency")
plt.show()
In [85]:
# Plot q-q plot of residuals
import pylab

import scipy.stats as stats

stats.probplot(residuals, dist = "norm", plot = pylab)

plt.show()

Observation:

• The histogram shows a roughly bell-shaped, symmetric distribution centered around 0, which is the expected shape for normally distributed residuals

• The KDE (Kernel Density Estimate) curve overlaid on the histogram closely follows a normal distribution shape, further supporting normality of the residuals

• There is a slight right skew visible in the tail, meaning there are a few observations where the model significantly underpredicted the actual house price, these are likely the high-value homes where MEDV was capped at 50, introducing some distortion

• The bulk of residuals fall within a narrow range of approximately -0.4 to +0.4, indicating that most predictions are fairly close to the actual log(MEDV) values with no extreme systematic errors

• This satisfies the fourth and final linear regression assumption, that the error terms are normally distributed, and when taken together with the previous three checks, confirms that model2 is a valid and well-specified linear regression model

Check the performance of the model on the train and test data set

In [86]:
# RMSE
def rmse(predictions, targets):
    return np.sqrt(((targets - predictions) ** 2).mean())


# MAPE
def mape(predictions, targets):
    return np.mean(np.abs((targets - predictions)) / targets) * 100


# MAE
def mae(predictions, targets):
    return np.mean(np.abs((targets - predictions)))


# Model Performance on test and train data
def model_pref(olsmodel, x_train, x_test):

    # In-sample Prediction
    y_pred_train = olsmodel.predict(x_train)
    y_observed_train = y_train

    # Prediction on test data
    y_pred_test = olsmodel.predict(x_test)
    y_observed_test = y_test

    print(
        pd.DataFrame(
            {
                "Data": ["Train", "Test"],
                "RMSE": [
                    rmse(y_pred_train, y_observed_train),
                    rmse(y_pred_test, y_observed_test),
                ],
                "MAE": [
                    mae(y_pred_train, y_observed_train),
                    mae(y_pred_test, y_observed_test),
                ],
                "MAPE": [
                    mape(y_pred_train, y_observed_train),
                    mape(y_pred_test, y_observed_test),
                ],
            }
        )
    )


# Checking model performance
model_pref(model2, X_train, X_test)

    Data      RMSE       MAE      MAPE
0  Train  0.195504  0.143686  4.981813
1   Test  0.198045  0.151284  5.257965

Observations on Model Performance:

• RMSE (Root Mean Squared Error)

o Train (0.1955) and Test (0.1980) are extremely close, with only a 0.0025 difference, indicating the model is not overfitting to the training data

o Since MEDV_log was the target, an RMSE of ~0.197 on the log scale translates to predictions that are off by roughly 1,000 – 2,000 in actual house price terms, which is acceptable

• MAE (Mean Absolute Error)

o Train (0.1437) and Test (0.1513) are again very close, meaning on average the model's predictions deviate by about 0.14–0.15 units on the log scale from the actual values. o MAE being lower than RMSE in both cases confirms there are no extreme outlier predictions dramatically inflating the error.

• MAPE (Mean Absolute Percentage Error)

o Train (4.98%) and (~5.26%) indicate the model predicts log(MEDV) within roughly 5% of the actual value on average, which is a strong result for a linear regression model. o The small gap of only 0.28% between train and test MAPE further confirms the model generalizes well to unseen data.

• Overall Assessment

o The near-identical metrics across train and test sets confirm the model is neither overfitting nor underfitting — it has found the right balance of complexity. o The consistency across all three metrics (RMSE, MAE, MAPE) tells the same story from three different angles, making this a robust and reliable model for predicting Boston house prices.

Apply cross validation to improve the model and evaluate it using different evaluation metrics

In [87]:
# Import the required function

from sklearn.model_selection import cross_val_score

# Build the regression model and cross-validate
linearregression = LinearRegression()

cv_Score11 = cross_val_score(linearregression, X_train, y_train, cv = 10)
cv_Score12 = cross_val_score(linearregression, X_train, y_train, cv = 10,
                             scoring = 'neg_mean_squared_error')


print("RSquared: %0.3f (+/- %0.3f)" % (cv_Score11.mean(), cv_Score11.std() * 2))
print("Mean Squared Error: %0.3f (+/- %0.3f)" % (-1*cv_Score12.mean(), cv_Score12.std() * 2))

RSquared: 0.729 (+/- 0.232)
Mean Squared Error: 0.041 (+/- 0.023)

Get model coefficients in a pandas DataFrame with column 'Feature' having all the features and column 'Coefs' with all the corresponding Coefs.

In [88]:
coef = model2.params

pd.DataFrame({'Feature' : coef.index, 'Coefs' : coef.values})
Out[88]:
Feature Coefs
0 const 4.649386
1 CRIM -0.012500
2 CHAS 0.119773
3 NOX -1.056225
4 RM 0.058907
5 DIS -0.044069
6 RAD 0.007848
7 PTRATIO -0.048504
8 LSTAT -0.029277

Conclusions:

Model Performance

• The final linear regression model (model2) achieved an R-squared of 0.767, meaning it explains approximately 77% of the variation in Boston median house prices using just 8 predictors, with a test MAPE of ~5.26% confirming strong generalization to unseen data.

• All four linear regression assumptions were satisfied, zero mean residuals, homoscedasticity (GQ test p-value = 0.302), linearity, and approximate normality of errors, validating model2 as a statistically sound and reliable model.

Key Findings from the Model

• The most influential negative drivers of house price are LSTAT (% lower-status population, coefficient -0.029), NOX (pollution, coefficient -1.056), and PTRATIO (pupil-teacher ratio, coefficient -0.049).

• The most influential positive drivers are CHAS (Charles River proximity, +12% price premium) and RM (number of rooms, ~5.9% per additional room).

• Variables TAX, ZN, INDUS, and AGE were either multicollinear or statistically insignificant and were appropriately removed without loss of model explanatory power.

Business Recommendations

1. Prioritize Pollution Reduction:

NOX had the single largest coefficient magnitude (-1.056), meaning pollution has a dramatic negative impact on property values. Municipal authorities and urban planners should prioritize relocating industrial activity away from residential zones and enforcing stricter emissions controls, even modest reductions in NOX levels could translate to meaningful appreciation in surrounding property values.

2. Invest in School Quality

The PTRATIO coefficient (-0.049) confirms that every additional student per teacher is associated with a ~4.9% drop in median home value. School districts and local governments that invest in hiring more teachers and reducing class sizes are likely to see a direct positive return in the form of higher property values and a stronger local tax base.

3. Target Low LSTAT Neighborhoods for Development

LSTAT is the strongest overall predictor of house price (-0.029 per percentage point). Developers and investors should focus on neighborhoods showing early signs of gentrification, areas where LSTAT is beginning to decline, as these are likely to experience the sharpest price appreciation over time.

4. Price River-Adjacent Properties at a Premium

The CHAS coefficient (+0.120) translates to approximately a 12% price premium for properties bordering the Charles River, all else being equal. Real estate agents and appraisers should consistently factor in river proximity when valuing properties, and developers should actively seek waterfront or near-waterfront parcels.

5. Room Count Remains a Reliable Value Driver

With each additional room associated with a ~5.9% increase in price, home extensions and renovations that add rooms offer a quantifiable return on investment. This is a straightforward lever for homeowners and property developers looking to increase asset value.

6. Be Cautious with Highway-Adjacent Properties

While RAD (highway accessibility) has a small positive coefficient (+0.008), its strong correlation with TAX (0.91) and the high tax rates in those areas means the price benefit of highway access may be partially offset by higher property tax burdens — buyers and investors should evaluate the net financial impact rather than treating accessibility as an unambiguous positive.

7. Model Limitation to Note

MEDV was capped at $50,000 in the original dataset (in 1970 dollars), meaning the model may underestimate prices for the highest-value properties. Any application of this model to current real estate markets should be treated as directional guidance only, and the model should ideally be retrained on more recent, uncapped data before being used for actual pricing or investment decisions.
